museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The year opens with award-show confidence, home-computer price pressure, crowded cartridge shelves, and a UK micro scene that feels more alive than the American console market feels stable.
museum.curatorNote: January 1983 is best read as a hinge. The North American crash was not a single-day event, and many games listed here are January shelf/context objects rather than exact launch-day claims.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five signs from the opening of a difficult, inventive year.

items.title: Electronic Games publishes the fourth Arkie Awards
items.body: The January issue of Electronic Games looked back at the previous prize year with confidence, even as the market underneath dedicated videogame publishing was beginning to wobble.
items.sourceNote: Electronic Games records identify the January 1983 issue with the fourth Arkie Awards.

items.title: Texas Instruments pushes the TI-99/4A hard
items.body: TI's home computer was selling in large numbers early in the year, but the price war with Commodore would soon turn volume into danger.
items.sourceNote: TI-99/4A histories describe January 1983 CES confidence and January price cuts.

items.title: The cartridge glut becomes visible
items.body: US retailers faced too many console games, too many publishers, and too little confidence in quality. From Britain, the crash arrived as news and cheap import lore rather than identical lived experience.
items.sourceNote: Video game crash summaries describe oversupply and declining consumer confidence through 1983.

items.title: UK software remains magazine-shaped
items.body: For Spectrum, BBC Micro and VIC-20 owners, the year's energy came from listings, small adverts, cassette inlays and the sense that anyone might publish a game.
items.sourceNote: UK magazine archives document C&VG, Your Computer and ZX Computing issues through 1983.

items.title: Arcades still look healthy from the floor
items.body: Pole Position, Q*bert, Joust and the late-1982 wave were still recent enough to make the arcade feel bright even while home-console economics dimmed.
items.sourceNote: 1982-83 arcade release records and 1983 arcade chart context.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight January shelf objects and 1983 candidates, dated cautiously.

items.title: Q*bert
items.body: A late-1982 release still feeling new on many floors, proof that character cabinets could still surprise players.
items.sourceNote: Q*bert release records are late 1982; January is shelf context.

items.title: Pole Position
items.body: Namco and Atari's racer remained the cabinet by which driving games were judged.
items.sourceNote: North American mass release was late November 1982.

items.title: Popeye
items.body: Nintendo's cartoon licence stayed visible in arcades, with chart strength reported in early 1983.
items.sourceNote: Popeye arcade chart references place it strongly in April 1983.

items.title: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial
items.body: The Christmas 1982 cartridge became part of the early-1983 returns-and-disappointment story.
items.sourceNote: E.T. released December 1982; later crash context is handled cautiously.

items.title: Pitfall!
items.body: Activision's 1982 classic remained the counterexample to the idea that every console cartridge had gone stale.
items.sourceNote: Pitfall! was released in September 1982.

items.title: The Hobbit
items.body: Melbourne House's adventure remained a UK conversation piece for text, graphics and patience.
items.sourceNote: Originally 1982; included for early-1983 UK shelf life.

items.title: Early Spectrum cassettes
items.body: Small publishers kept feeding the young machine with games cheap enough to imagine buying with pocket money.
items.sourceNote: Specific January title dates not asserted.

items.title: Arcade ports in magazines
items.body: Readers often met games as screenshots, listings and promises before they met finished software.
items.sourceNote: UK print culture context.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The display case is a price war, a console hangover and a British bedroom computer.

items.title: TI-99/4A
items.body: A technically interesting home computer being pulled into destructive discounting.
items.sourceNote: TI-99/4A timelines describe January 1983 price pressure.

items.title: Commodore 64
items.body: The C64's price aggression helped make home computers feel like a better games value than many consoles.
items.sourceNote: C64 price-war context through 1983.

items.title: Atari 2600
items.body: Still everywhere, but now haunted by shelf overload and falling confidence.
items.sourceNote: Crash histories.

items.title: ZX Spectrum
items.body: In Britain, the Spectrum carried a different mood: cheaper software, local creators and the romance of cassette loading.
items.sourceNote: UK microcomputer context.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Arcade and micro coverage for UK readers
items.curatorNote: C&VG connected coin-op glamour to the machines appearing under British televisions.
items.body: C&VG connected coin-op glamour to the machines appearing under British televisions.
items.sourceNote: Specific cover line not asserted.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: Home computing as a games workshop
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for January 1983.
items.curatorNote: A broader computer magazine that still mattered to players because listings and adverts were software discovery.
items.body: A broader computer magazine that still mattered to players because listings and adverts were software discovery.
items.sourceNote: Computing History lists Your Computer 1983 issues.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: The Spectrum shelf takes shape
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for early 1983.
items.curatorNote: ZX-specific magazines made a young machine feel like a whole culture.
items.body: ZX-specific magazines made a young machine feel like a whole culture.
items.sourceNote: World of Spectrum archives list ZX Computing issues in 1983.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: The network was still paper, shops, schools and clubs.

items.title: The letters page carried rumours
items.body: Readers learned what mattered from magazine columns and other readers' complaints.

items.title: Listings were downloadable by hand
items.body: Typing code was slow, social and error-prone, but it made software feel graspable.

items.title: Shop windows were search results
items.body: A boxed cassette or discount cartridge could redirect a whole weekend.

items.title: Bulletin boards were specialist
items.body: Online access existed, but not as everyday gaming life for most UK players.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: January felt like a year trying to hold two truths at once.

items.title: The arcade still had glamour
items.body: Cabinets looked confident even as the business pages darkened.

items.title: Home computers felt like escape routes
items.body: A Spectrum or C64 promised games and agency, not just another sealed cartridge.

items.title: The US crash was distant but audible
items.body: British players heard the rumble through magazines, prices and imported stories.

items.title: Print made the future monthly
items.body: Every issue seemed to reveal another machine, language, cassette or warning sign.
